name: Laser Classification Decision Tree
description: Interactive decision tree for determining laser classification according to IEC 60825-1

wavelength_check.question: What is the laser wavelength?
wavelength_check.explanation: Wavelength determines which eye structures are affected and which AEL tables apply.

options.label: UV (180-400 nm)
options.description: Corneal hazard - absorbed by eye surface

options.label: Visible (400-700 nm)
options.description: Retinal hazard - focused on retina, blink reflex possible

options.label: Near-IR (700-1400 nm)
options.description: Retinal hazard - invisible, no blink reflex

enclosure_check.question: Is the laser fully enclosed with interlocks?
enclosure_check.explanation: Enclosed lasers with proper interlocks may qualify as Class 1 even if they contain high-power sources.

options.label: Yes, fully enclosed with safety interlocks
options.description: No accessible radiation during normal operation

result.explanation: Fully enclosed lasers with interlocks prevent any accessible emission during normal operation.

power_check.question: What is the accessible output power (CW) or energy (pulsed)?
power_check.explanation: Compare the accessible emission to the AEL tables for the identified wavelength.

divergence_check.question: What is the beam divergence?
divergence_check.explanation: Divergence affects whether optical instruments can collect hazardous amounts of radiation.

m_classification_threshold.explanation: Beams with divergence >1.5 mrad may qualify for M-suffix

optics_check.question: Could optical instruments be used to view the beam?
optics_check.explanation: If optical instruments can increase the hazard, an M-suffix classification may be appropriate.

options.label: No, situation prevents optical instrument use

practice_scenarios.title: Laser Pointer Evaluation
practice_scenarios.description: A consumer laser pointer is rated at 5 mW output, 650 nm wavelength, with 1 mrad divergence.
hints: 5 mW is exactly at the Class 3R boundary
practice_scenarios.explanation: This is a Class 3R laser because: (1) 650 nm is visible, (2) 5 mW equals the Class 3R AEL limit, (3) Low divergence means no M-suffix needed.

practice_scenarios.title: Enclosed Industrial Laser
practice_scenarios.description: A 50W CO2 laser (10600 nm) is installed in a manufacturing system with complete enclosure and safety interlocks.
hints: High power doesn't matter if properly enclosed
practice_scenarios.explanation: Despite containing a high-power Class 4 source, proper enclosure with interlocks makes this a Class 1 product during normal operation.

practice_scenarios.title: Fiber Optic Source
practice_scenarios.description: A fiber-coupled 980 nm diode laser outputs 100 mW with 0.2 NA divergence (approximately 11.5° cone angle).
hints: Near-IR wavelength - no blink reflex protection
Calculate if divergence qualifies for M-suffix
practice_scenarios.explanation: At 100 mW near-IR, this exceeds Class 3R limits. The high divergence may qualify for 1M if optical instruments are a concern, otherwise Class 3B.

correct_classification.message: Correct! You've properly applied the IEC 60825-1 classification criteria.
correct_classification.reinforcement: Remember that enclosure and interlocks can override high-power classification.

incorrect_classification.message: That's not quite right. Let's review the decision path.
incorrect_classification.guidance: Check: (1) Is the wavelength in the retinal hazard region? (2) Does enclosure apply? (3) Compare power to AEL tables.

common_mistakes.mistake: Assuming high power always equals Class 4
common_mistakes.correction: Enclosed systems can be Class 1 regardless of internal power
High divergence + optical instrument access = M-classification

common_mistakes.mistake: Applying blink reflex to all wavelengths
common_mistakes.correction: Blink reflex only protects against visible light (400-700 nm)
